Problem 1

Problem 2
Greg wants to buy a new car. He looks at two different models. Car A costs $40,000, and he estimates it will cost $2,000 per year for gas and maintenance. Car B costs $60,000, and he estimates it will cost about $1,000 per year for gas and maintenance. Under what circumstances would it make sense for Greg to buy Car A? Under what circumstances would it make sense for Greg to buy Car B? Justify your answer.

Fishing Adventures rents small fishing boats to tourists for day-long fishing trips. Each boat can hold at most eight people. Additionally, each boat can only carry 1,200 pounds of people and gear for safety reasons. Assume an average an adult weighs 150 pounds and a child weighs 75 pounds. Also assume each group will require 200 pounds of gear plus 10 pounds of gear per person.
Write an inequality that illustrates the weight limit for a group of adults and children on the fishing boat and a second inequality that represents the total number of passengers in the fishing boat. Graph and identify at least one solution to the system of inequalities.
Several groups of people wish to rent a boat. Group 1 has 4 adults and 2 children. Group 2 has 3 adults and 5 children. Group 3 has 8 adults. Which of the groups, if any, can safely rent a boat? What other combinations of adults and children are possible?

Problem 1
Lisa is working with the system of equations
$${x+2y=7}$$
and
$${2x-5y=5}$$
. She multiplies the first equation by
$$2$$
and then subtracts the second equation to find
$${9y=9}$$
, telling her that
$${y=1}$$
. Lisa then finds that
$${x=5}$$
.  Thinking about this procedure, Lisa wonders:
There are lots of ways I could go about solving this problem. I could add 5 times the first equation and twice the second, or I could multiply the first equation by
$$-2$$
and add the second. I seem to find that there is only one solution to the two equations, but I wonder if I will get the same solution if I use a different method?
What is the answer to Lisa's question? Explain.
Does the answer to (1) change if we have a system of two equations in two unknowns with no solutions? What if there are infinitely many solutions?

Task
Ivan’s furnace has quit working during the coldest part of the year, and he is eager to get it fixed. He decides to call some mechanics and furnace specialists to see what it might cost him to have the furnace fixed. Since he is unsure of the parts he needs, he decides to compare the costs based only on service fees and labor costs. Shown below are the price estimates for labor that were given to him by three different companies. Each company has given the same time estimate for fixing the furnace.
Company A charges \$35 per hour to its customers.
Company B charges a \$20 service fee for coming out to the house and then \$25 per hour for each additional hour.
Company C charges a \$45 service fee for coming out to the house and then \$20 per hour for each additional hour.
For which time intervals should Ivan choose Company A, Company B, Company C? Support your decision with sound reasoning and representations. Consider including equations, tables, and graphs.

khan_exercises.title: Slope-intercept intro
khan_exercises.description: Practice your basic understanding of slope-intercept form for linear equations.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of $y=5x-1$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The equation is in *slope intercept form*, $y=mx+b$.

In this form, $m$ gives the slope of the line.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The slope of $y=\maroonC 5x-1$ is $\maroonC 5$.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of $y=-4x-3$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The equation is in *slope intercept form*, $y=mx+b$.

In this form, $m$ gives the slope of the line.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The slope of $y=\maroonC {-4}x-3$ is $\maroonC {-4}$.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of $y=6x+2$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The equation is in *slope intercept form*, $y=mx+b$.

In this form, $m$ gives the slope of the line.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The slope of $y=\maroonC {6}x+2$ is $\maroonC {6}$.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of $y=4-2x$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The equation is in *slope intercept form*, $y=mx+b$.

In this form, $m$ gives the slope of the line.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
The slope of $y=4\maroonC {-2}x$ is $\maroonC {-2}$.

items.question_content:
**Find the equation of the line.**  
*Use exact numbers. For a negative y-intercept, type a negative sign.*

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$x+$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: We are asked to complete the equation in *slope-intercept form* : $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, $m$ gives us the slope of the line and $b$ gives us its $y$-intercept.

By looking at the graph, we can see that the $y$-intercept is $(0,3)$.

In order to find the slope, we need another point on the line  whose coordinates are clearly visible. Such a point is $(1,6)$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Now, to find the slope, we need to take the ratio of the corresponding differences in the $y$-values and the $x$-values:

$\dfrac{6-3}{1-0}=\dfrac{3}{1}=3$
The equation is $y=3x+3$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes each scale by one. A graph of a line goes through the points zero, three and one, six.

answers.message: You are close, but you should arrive at the exact value.

khan_exercises.title: Slope from equation
khan_exercises.description: Find the slopes of lines whose equations are given in various forms. For example, find the slope of 2x+3y=5.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of the line?**

$3x-4y=7$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can determine the slope of the graph by bringing the equation to *slope-intercept form*. So let’s solve the equation for $y$:

$\begin{align}3x-4y&=7
\\\\
3x-7&=4y
\\\\
\dfrac{3}{4}x-\dfrac{7}{4}&=y
\end{align}$
Now we have the equation in *slope-intercept form*: $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, the slope is simply the coefficient of $x$, meaning the value of $m$.
The slope is $\dfrac34$.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of the line?**

$2x-5y=9$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can determine the slope of the graph by bringing the equation to *slope-intercept form*. So let’s solve the equation for $y$:

$\begin{align}2x-5y&=9
\\\\
2x-9&=5y
\\\\
\dfrac25x-\dfrac95&=y\end{align}$
Now we have the equation in *slope-intercept form*: $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, the slope is simply the coefficient of $x$, meaning the value of $m$.
The slope is $\dfrac25$.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of the line?**

$8x-6y=1$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can determine the slope of the graph by bringing the equation to *slope-intercept form*. So let’s solve the equation for $y$:

$\begin{align}8x-6y&=1
\\\\
8x-1&=6y
\\\\
\dfrac{8}{6}x-\dfrac{1}{6}&=y
\\\\
\dfrac{4}{3}x-\dfrac{1}{6}&=y\end{align}$
Now we have the equation in *slope-intercept form*: $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, the slope is simply the coefficient of $x$, meaning the value of $m$.
The slope is $\dfrac{4}{3}$.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of the line?**

$x+3y=10$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can determine the slope of the graph by bringing the equation to *slope-intercept form*. So let’s solve the equation for $y$:

$\begin{align}x+3y&=10
\\\\
3y&=10-x
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{10}{3}-\dfrac{1}{3}x
\end{align}$
Now we have the equation in *slope-intercept form*: $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, the slope is simply the coefficient of $x$, meaning the value of $m$.
The slope is $-\dfrac{1}{3}$.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of the line?**

$7x+2y=5$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can determine the slope of the graph by bringing the equation to *slope-intercept form*. So let’s solve the equation for $y$:

$\begin{align}7x+2y&=5
\\\\
2y&=5-7x
\\\\
y&=\dfrac52-\dfrac72x\end{align}$
Now we have the equation in *slope-intercept form*: $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, the slope is simply the coefficient of $x$, meaning the value of $m$.
The slope is $-\dfrac72$.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of the line?**

$6x+10y=8$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can determine the slope of the graph by bringing the equation to *slope-intercept form*. So let’s solve the equation for $y$:

$\begin{align}6x+10y&=8
\\\\
10y&=8-6x
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{8}{10}-\dfrac{6}{10}x
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{8}{10}-\dfrac35x\end{align}$
Now we have the equation in *slope-intercept form*: $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, the slope is simply the coefficient of $x$, meaning the value of $m$.
The slope is $-\dfrac35$.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of the line?**

$2x+4y=6x-y$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can determine the slope of the graph by bringing the equation to *slope-intercept form*. So let’s solve the equation for $y$:

$\begin{align}2x+4y&=6x-y
\\\\
4y+y&=6x-2x
\\\\
5y&=4x
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{4}{5}x
\end{align}$
Now we have the equation in *slope-intercept form*: $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, the slope is simply the coefficient of $x$, meaning the value of $m$.
The slope is $\dfrac45$.

items.question_content:
**Find the equation of the line.**  
*Use exact numbers. For a negative y-intercept, type a negative sign.*

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$x+$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: We are asked to complete the equation in *slope-intercept form* : $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, $m$ gives us the slope of the line and $b$ gives us its $y$-intercept.

By looking at the graph, we can see that the $y$-intercept is $(0,3)$.

In order to find the slope, we need another point on the line  whose coordinates are clearly visible. Such a point is $(1,6)$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Now, to find the slope, we need to take the ratio of the corresponding differences in the $y$-values and the $x$-values:

$\dfrac{6-3}{1-0}=\dfrac{3}{1}=3$
The equation is $y=3x+3$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. The x- and y-axes each scale by one. A graph of a line goes through the points zero, three and one, six.

answers.message: You are close, but you should arrive at the exact value.

hints: We are asked to complete the equation in *slope-intercept form* : $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, $m$ gives us the slope of the line and $b$ gives us its $y$-intercept.

By looking at the graph, we can see that the $y$-intercept is $(0,3)$.

In order to find the slope, we need another point on the line whose coordinates are clearly visible. Such a point is $(2,0)$.



[[☃ image 1]]

Now, to find the slope, we need to take the ratio of the corresponding differences in the $y$-values and the $x$-values:

$\dfrac{0-3}{2-0}=\dfrac{-3}{2}=-\dfrac{3}{2}$
The equation is $y=-\dfrac{3}{2}x+3$.

khan_exercises.title: Slope from equation
khan_exercises.description: Find the slopes of lines whose equations are given in various forms. For example, find the slope of 2x+3y=5.

items.question_content:
**What is the slope of the line?**

$3x-4y=7$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can determine the slope of the graph by bringing the equation to *slope-intercept form*. So let’s solve the equation for $y$:

$\begin{align}3x-4y&=7
\\\\
3x-7&=4y
\\\\
\dfrac{3}{4}x-\dfrac{7}{4}&=y
\end{align}$
Now we have the equation in *slope-intercept form*: $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, the slope is simply the coefficient of $x$, meaning the value of $m$.
The slope is $\dfrac34$.

items.question_content:
Liz's math test included a survey question asking how many hours students spent studying for the test. The scatterplot and regression line below show the relationship between how many hours students spent studying and their score on the test.

The fitted line has a slope of $15$.

**What is the best interpretation of this slope?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

![A graph plots Score, from 0 to 90, in increments of 10, versus Study time, in hours, from 0 to 4.5, in increments of 0.5, on an x y plane. Dozens of points rise diagonally in a tight, narrow cluster between (1.8, 62) and (4.4, 95). A regression line rises diagonally through the center of the cluster from (0, 38) to (4.2, 100). All values estimated.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e4af33ae2251e57b57961bea23db4626438869a4)
hints: The slope of the regression line represents the average change in the response ($y$) variable that's associated with a one-unit change in the explanatory ($x$) variable.

In this case, the slope represents the average change in score associated with a one hour increase in study time.

$\text{slope}=\dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{run}}=\dfrac{15}{1}=\dfrac{\text{change in score }(y)}{\text{change in study time }(x)}$
The model predicts that the score will increase $15$ points for each additional hour of study time.

choices.content: The model predicts that students who scored $0$ studied for an average of $15$ hours.
The model predicts that students who didn't study at all will have an average score of $15$ points.
The model predicts that the score will increase $15$ points for each additional hour of study time.
The model predicts that the study time will increase $15$ hours for each additional point scored.

khan_exercises.title: Graph from slope-intercept form
khan_exercises.description: Practice drawing the graph of a line given in slope-intercept form. For example, graph y = 3x + 2.
topic_path: Topic C: Slope and equations of lines

items.question_content:
**Graph $y=-2x+5$.**

[[☃ grapher 1]]
hints:
The equation is in *slope-intercept form* : $y=m\!\cdot\!x+b$. In this form, $m$ gives us the slope of the line and $b$ gives us its $y$-intercept.

So $y=-2x+5$ has a slope of $-2$ and a $y$-intercept at $(0,5)$.

We need two points. We already have the $y$-intercept $(0,5)$.

We can find a second point by reasoning about the slope. A slope of $-2$ means that when the $x$-value *increases* by $\purple{1}$, the $y$-value *decreases* by $\green{2}$. 

$(0 \purple{+1},5 \green{-2})=(1,3)$



[[☃ image 1]]

Now we can graph the equation.

[[☃ grapher 1]]
